Advisor: Got it. We’ll hit income, spending, what you’ve got saved, and any debts or insurance. Going back to what you said—“moving parts”—let’s start with income so I’m not guessing. Roughly what’s the household gross income per year, all-in?
Client 2: Like… a hundred and twenty-ish?
Client 1: No—sorry, not that high. It’s about $111,550 a year gross, if we’re counting everything the way we did last time.
Advisor: Perfect, thank you. Emily, when you said “120-ish,” you were just rounding up?
Client 2: Yeah, I was ballparking. Elizabeth has the real number.

Client 2: Hey—okay, fair. I do.
Advisor: No worries, it happens all the time. Switching to spending: about what do your total monthly expenses run—mortgage or rent, utilities, food, everything?
Client 1: Around $3,000 a month. That’s our baseline spend.
Advisor: $3,000 a month—got it. Is that net of taxes, like what leaves the checking account, or are you thinking gross?
Client 1: Net—like what we actually pay out.

Advisor: Got it. We’ll hit income, spending, what you’ve got saved, and any debts or insurance. Going back to what you said—“moving parts”—let’s start with income so I’m not guessing. Roughly what’s the household gross income per year, all-in?
Client 2: Like… a hundred and twenty-ish?
Client 1: No—sorry, not that high. It’s about $111,550 a year gross, if we’re counting everything the way we did last time.
Advisor: Perfect, thank you. Emily, when you said “120-ish,” you were just rounding up?
Client 2: Yeah, I was ballparking. Elizabeth has the real number.

Advisor: Great—so no liabilities showing up, which matches what you’re saying. Now let’s talk assets—what you’ve built up. Roughly, total investable assets across accounts—brokerage, retirement, cash—what’s the combined value?
Client 2: It’s like… four-fifty? Four-sixty?
Client 1: It’s $463,850 total investable, last time we added it up.
Advisor: Perfect—$463,850. Emily, you were basically right, just a little low.
Client 2: Yeah, I was rounding.

Advisor: And you also have something in private markets through an insurance provider—Emily, is that yours—around $40,850?
Client 2: Yep, that’s mine. About $40,850.
Advisor: Great. And then the home—primary residence—valued around $298,300, held jointly, showing on the advisor platform. That sound right?
Client 1: Yes, about $298,300.
Client 2: Yeah.

Client 2: Busy, but good. Okay—yeah.
Advisor: Busy week—yeah, I hear you. Okay, going back to what you said earlier about paying cards off monthly, that helps a lot.
Advisor: Let’s zoom out for a second on the household basics. You two are married, right—Elizabeth and Emily?
Client 1: Yes, we’re married.
Client 2: Yep, married.

Client 1: None—no dependents.
Advisor: Got it—zero dependents. I’ll keep it simple.
Advisor: Now, earlier you both sounded pretty comfortable with investing risk, but I want to label it correctly. Would you say your overall risk tolerance is “growth”—meaning you’re okay with ups and downs for higher long-term return?
Client 2: Yeah, growth. That’s us.
Client 1: Growth, yes—within reason, but growth.

Client 2: Yeah, two adults.
Client 1: Two adults, yes.
Advisor: Perfect. Now, I’m going to ask a slightly “industry” question—bear with me. Are you working with me through a US RIA setup, like a registered investment advisor here in the States?
Client 2: Uh, I think so? Like, you’re an advisor, so yes.
Client 1: Yes—US RIA. That’s how your firm is set up, right?

Advisor: Hi Elizabeth, hi Emily—good to meet you both. I’m glad we could get this on the calendar. Before we dive in, how are you two doing today?
Client 1: Doing okay—busy, but okay. I’m Elizabeth, I tend to be the spreadsheet person.
Client 2: And I’m Emily. I’m good—sorry, I might jump in a lot.
Advisor: Totally fine. If you do, I’ll just slow us down when we need a number. So, Elizabeth, what made you reach out now?
Client 1: We just want to make sure we’re not missing anything—retirement, taxes, all of it. It feels like we have a lot of moving parts.

Client 1: Yes—US RIA. That’s how your firm is set up, right?
Advisor: Exactly, yes—US RIA. Thanks, Elizabeth.
Advisor: And then taxes—earlier you mentioned being sensitive to the bracket. Do you consider yourselves in the 35% federal bracket right now?
Client 2: I thought we were like 32%.
Client 1: No—Emily, we’re in the 35% bracket. We hit that last year.

Advisor: Okay—$111,550 gross household. And just to anchor it, Elizabeth, your W-2 is about $84,500 a year, right?
Client 1: Yes, $84,500.
Advisor: And Emily, yours is around $27,100 gross from work?
Client 2: Yep, about $27,100.
Advisor: Great. Now, I also want to capture the other income streams you mentioned in your intake—there’s a pension coming in monthly?

Advisor: Okay—thanks. Going back to what you said earlier about the investment income, Emily, I want to make sure I’ve got your basics right before we talk strategy.
Client 2: Sure, yeah.
Advisor: First, just confirming roles in the household—Elizabeth, you’re the primary, and Emily, you’re the spouse/partner on the plan. Is that how you two think about it?
Client 1: Yes, I’m the primary on most of the accounts and paperwork.
Client 2: And I’m the spouse, yeah.

Client 1: Yes, I’m the primary on most of the accounts and paperwork.
Client 2: And I’m the spouse, yeah.

Client 2: Business owner. I do some consulting and run my own thing.
Client 1: Yeah—she’s not W-2. It’s her business.

Client 1: And I’m Client 1, yep.

Client 2: February 3rd, 1990.

Client 1: Yes, that’s fine.
